column2: 2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#ADR.OR.A.005 Scope#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#This Annex establishes the requirements to be followed by:#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(a)an aerodrome operator subject to Regulation (EC) No 216/2008 with respect to its certification
column3: management
column4: manuals and other responsibilities; and#

column2: 2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(b)a provider of apron management services.#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#ADR.OR.A.010 Competent Authority#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#For the purpose of this Part
column3: the Competent Authority shall be the one designated by the Member State where the aerodrome is located.#

column2: 2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#ADR.OR.A.015 Means of compliance#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#

2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(a)Alternative means of compliance to those adopted by the Agency may be used by an aerodrome operator or an apron management service provider to establish compliance with Regulation (EC) No 216/2008 and its Implementing Rules.#

2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(b)When an aerodrome operator or an apron management service provider wishes to use an alternative means of compliance to the Acceptable Means of Compliance (AMC) adopted by the Agency to establish compliance with Regulation (EC) No 216/2008 and its Implementing Rules
column3: it shall
column4: prior to implementing it
column5: provide the Competent Authority with a full description of the alternative means of compliance. The description shall include any revisions to manuals or procedures that may be relevant
column6: as well as an assessment demonstrating that the Implementing Rules are met.#

column2: 2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#The aerodrome operator or the provider of apron management services may implement these alternative means of compliance subject to prior approval by the Competent Authority and upon receipt of the notification
column3: as prescribed in ADR.AR.A.015(d).#

column2: 2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(c)Where apron management services are not provided by the aerodrome operator itself
column3: the use of alternative means of compliance by providers of such services in accordance with (a) and (b)
column4: shall also require prior agreement by the operator of the aerodrome where such services are provided.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#AMC1 ADR.OR.A.015 Means of compliance#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#DEMONSTRATION OF COMPLIANCE#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#In order to demonstrate that the Implementing Rules are met
column3: a safety (risk) assessment should be completed and documented. The result of this safety (risk) assessment should demonstrate that an equivalent level of safety to that established by the Acceptable Means of Compliance (AMC) adopted by the Agency is reached.#

column1: ADR.OR.B.005 Certification obligations of aerodromes and aerodrome operators#ERULES-1963177438-2087#Aerodromes;#6 March
column2: 2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#Prior to commencing the operation of an aerodrome or when an exemption in accordance with Article 5 has been revoked
column3: the aerodrome operator shall obtain the applicable certificate(s) issued by the Competent Authority.#

column2: 2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#ADR.OR.B.015 Application for a certificate#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(a)The application for a certificate shall be made in a form and manner established by the Competent Authority.#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(b)The applicant shall provide the Competent Authority with the following:#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(1)its official name and business name
column3: address
column4: and mailing address;#

column2: 2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(2)information and data regarding:#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(i)the location of the aerodrome;#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(ii)the type of operations at the aerodrome and the associated airspace; and#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(iii)the design and facilities of the aerodrome
column3: in accordance with the applicable certification specifications established by the Agency;#

column2: 2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(3)any proposed deviations from the identified applicable certification specifications established by the Agency;#

2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(4)documentation demonstrating how it will comply with the applicable requirements established in Regulation (EC) No 216/2008 and its Implementing Rules. Such documentation shall include a procedure
column3: contained in the aerodrome manual
column4: describing how changes not requiring prior approval will be managed and notified to the Competent Authority; subsequent changes to this procedure shall require prior approval by the Competent Authority;#

column2: 2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(5)evidence of adequacy of resources to operate the aerodrome in accordance with the applicable requirements;#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(6)documented evidence showing the relationship of the applicant with the aerodrome owner and/or the land owner;#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(7)the name of and relevant information about the accountable manager and the other nominated persons required by ADR.OR.D.015; and#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(8)a copy of the aerodrome manual required by ADR.OR.E.005.#
2014#Implementing Regulation (EU) 2020/469#(c)If acceptable to the Competent Authority
column3: information under points (7) and (8) may be provided at a later stage determined by the Competent Authority
column4: but prior to the issuance of the certificate.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#GM1 ADR.OR.B.040(f) Changes#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ASSESSMENT OF CHANGES#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(a)Safety assessment for a change#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#A safety assessment for a change should include:#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)identification of the scope of the change;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)identification of hazards;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(3)determination of the safety criteria applicable to the change;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(4)risk analysis in relation to the harmful effects or improvements in safety related to the change;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(5)risk evaluation and
column3: if required
column4: risk mitigation for the change to meet the applicable safety criteria;#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(6)verification that the change conforms to the scope that was subject to safety assessment
column3: and meets the safety criteria
column4: before the change is put into operation; and#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(7)the specification of the monitoring requirements necessary to ensure that the aerodrome and its operation will continue to meet the safety criteria after the change has taken place.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(b)Scope of the safety assessment#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#The scope of the safety assessment should include the following elements and their interaction:#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)the aerodrome
column3: its operation
column4: management

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)interfaces and interactions between the elements being changed and the remainder of the system;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(3)interfaces and interactions between the elements being changed and the environment in which it is intended to operate; and#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(4)the full lifecycle of the change from definition to operations.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(c)Safety criteria#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#The safety criteria used should be defined in accordance with the procedures for the management of change contained in the aerodrome manual.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#The safety criteria used should
column3: depending on the availability of data
column4: be specified with reference to explicit quantitative acceptable safety risk levels
column7: the safety performance of the existing system

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#AMC1 ADR.OR.D.005(b)(1) Management system#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#SAFETY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM#

2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#The safety management system of an aerodrome operator should encompass safety by establishing an organisational structure for the management of safety proportionate and appropriate to the size of the aerodrome operator
column3: and the nature and type of operations. The organisational structure should include a Safety Review Board
column4: and depending on its organisational complexity and structure
column5: a Safety Services Office to assist the work of the safety manager
column6: in accordance with paragraph (a) and (b) below:#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(a)Safety Services Office#

2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)The safety manager (see ADR.OR.D.015 and AMC1 ADR.OR.D.015(c)) should be responsible for the operation of the Safety Services Office which should be independent and neutral in terms of the processes and decisions made regarding the delivery of services by the line managers of operational units.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)The function of the Safety Services Office should be to:#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(i)manage and oversee the hazard identification system;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(ii)monitor safety performance of operational units directly involved in aerodrome operations;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(iii)advise senior management on safety management matters; and#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(iv)assist line managers with safety management matters.#

2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(3)Operators of multiple aerodromes should either establish a central Safety Services Office and appropriate safety departments/functions at all aerodromes or separate Safety Services Office at each aerodrome. Arrangements should be made to ensure continuous flow of information and adequate coordination.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(b)Safety Review Board#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)The Safety Review Board should be a high level committee that considers matters of strategic safety in support of the accountable manager’s safety accountability.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)The Safety Review Board should be chaired by the accountable manager
column3: and be composed of heads of functional areas.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(3)The Safety Review Board should monitor:#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(i)safety performance against the safety policy and objectives;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(ii)that any safety action is taken in a timely manner; and#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(iii)the effectiveness of the organisation’s safety management processes.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(4)The Safety Review Board should ensure that appropriate resources are allocated to achieve the established safety performance.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(5)The safety manager or any other relevant person may attend
column3: as appropriate
column4: Safety Review Board meetings. He/she may communicate to the accountable manager all information
column6: to allow decision making based on safety data.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(6)Operators of multiple aerodromes should either establish a central Safety Review Board
column3: or separate Safety Review Boards for each aerodrome or group of aerodromes. In the case of central or group Safety Review Groups
column4: they should ensure that all aerodromes are represented in the Safety Review Board
column5: at the appropriate management level. Arrangements should be made to ensure continuous flow of information and adequate coordination.#

column3: the aerodrome operator should nominate a person who fulfils the role of safety manager
column4: and who is responsible for coordinating the safety management system (see ADR.OR.D.015 and AMC1 ADR.OR.D.015(c)).#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(1)The purpose of the training programme is to ensure the initial and continued competence of the aerodrome operator’s personnel to perform their duties
column3: irrespective of their level in the organisation of the aerodrome. This is to be achieved by ensuring that each person receives initial
column4: recurrent
column6: refresher training (as a result of absence from duties)
column7: and other ad hoc training (e.g. due to the planned/implemented change to the aerodrome system
column9: which is relevant to his or her function and tasks.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#One method to achieve this is by identifying the tasks of the employees and ensuring that each one of them receives the training that corresponds to such tasks.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#For example
column3: an employee whose tasks involve driving would need to undergo the relevant driving training (depending on the area of the aerodrome that he or she will operate in the course of his or her duties). Moreover
column4: if the duties of that person involve also conducting inspections of the movement area
column5: then the training plan for that employee would also need to include relevant training for that area.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#Moreover
column3: apart from this specific type of training
column4: one would also consider the need for all personnel to undergo a safety management system training relevant to their duties.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(2)As part of the process of the development of the training programme
column3: there are also other issues that need to be considered
column4: such as:#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(i)the content and duration of the courses;#

column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(ii)the minimum performance required by the trainees;#

column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(iii)the method to be used for the delivery of the training;#

column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(iv)the way the competency assessments of the trainees will be performed; and#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(v)the development of the relevant procedures to ensure that the training programme achieves its objectives and covers circumstances that may occur (e.g. the case where an employee fails to achieve the required competency level).#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#Consideration needs to be given to the fact that the relevant procedures would also need to address the organisation and implementation of the recurrent
column3: refresher and continuation training.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(b)Personnel of other organisations operating or providing services at the aerodrome#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(1)At the aerodrome environment
column3: there may be also other organisations that operate or provide their services to other organisations or the aerodrome operator. Given that such activities may have an effect on safety and affect aerodrome operations
column4: it is necessary to ensure that the personnel of these organisations that are allowed unescorted access to the movement area and other operational areas of the aerodrome
column5: are trained in the applicable aerodrome’s operational procedures and requirements
column6: so that they are enabled to safely operate within the aerodrome environment (e.g. the apron
column7: and sometimes on the manoeuvring area)
column8: sharing a common understanding about safety management.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#To achieve this
column3: the aerodrome operator needs to identify which are the applicable operational procedures and requirements for the relevant training of this type of personnel.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#For instance
column3: if an employee of another organisation needs to drive and work on the apron or on the manoeuvring area
column4: then he or she would need to undergo the relevant training prior to being allowed to do so. This would involve the relevant driving training. On the other hand
column5: the same person would be subject to certain aerodrome operational procedures and requirements which apply on this area
column6: and so he or she would need to also receive training on these procedures and requirements (e.g. as part of the FOD control programme). Moreover
column7: there is also an additional need for this employee to receive a safety management system training relevant to their duties
column8: e.g. how to file an occurrence report
column9: etc.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(2)As part of this process
column3: the aerodrome operator would need to make considerations similar to the ones referred to in (a)(2) above
column4: in order to ensure the continued competence and awareness of such personnel in the required fields.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(3)In this way
column3: the aerodrome operator is expected to be able to safely manage the interfaces it has with the other organisations present at the aerodrome. However
column4: in order to ensure the successful implementation of this training
column5: the aerodrome operator would need to cooperate and have relevant arrangements with the organisations whose employees need to complete this training. Depending on the situation and the solutions chosen in each case
column6: the content of the arrangements may vary and cover different areas
column7: such as the exchange of relevant information regarding absence of personnel for the organisation of a refresher training
column8: the use of instructors or assessors proposed by these organisations for the implementation of the relevant part of the training programme
column9: etc.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(4)It is to be noted that the training related to the performance of the actual duties of this type of personnel (e.g. how to use its own organisation’s equipment
column3: or the internal procedures of that organisation
column4: etc.) falls outside of the scope of this part of the training required under ADR.OR.D.017.#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#(c)Understanding of the aerodrome system as a whole#

column1: GM1 ADR.OR.D.017(a);(b);(c);(d) Training and proficiency check programmes#ERULES-1963177438-18539#Aerodromes;#5 March
column2: 2021#ED Decision 2021/003/R#It is important that the training provides all trainees with an understanding of the interdependencies between the various functions and actors at the aerodrome environment
column3: and how their work is related to the functions and work of other personnel and organisations
column4: so that they acquire a rather ‘global’ (as opposed to an ‘isolated’) perspective.#

column11: but they may not be appropriate for determining how a person would react in an operational situation. A written assessment may also be computer-based.#

2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#ADR.OR.D.020 Facilities requirements#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#

2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(a)The aerodrome operator shall ensure that adequate and appropriate facilities are available to its personnel or personnel employed by parties with whom it has contracted for the provision of aerodrome operational and maintenance services.#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(b)The aerodrome operator shall designate appropriate areas at the aerodrome to be used for the storage of dangerous goods transported through the aerodrome
column3: in accordance with the Technical Instructions.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#GM1 ADR.OR.D.020(a) Facilities requirements#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#FACILITIES TO BE PROVIDED#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#Facilities should be provided to allow the performance of all tasks and activities in accordance with the applicable requirements. This includes
column3: but is not limited to:#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(a)adequate offices
column3: working space
column4: and office equipment;#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(b)personnel protective equipment;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(c)equipment necessary for inspecting the aerodrome and its facilities
column3: such as clinometers
column4: distance measurement devices

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(d)access to data sources necessary for the development and effective functioning of the safety management system and compliance monitoring of the aerodrome.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#AMC1 ADR.OR.D.020(b) Facilities requirements#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#Designated areas may vary and include facilities such as cargo areas
column3: or even open-air areas.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#Aircraft stands should also be designated for aircrafts that carry dangerous goods.#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#ADR.OR.D.025 Coordination with other organisations#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#The aerodrome operator shall:#

2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(a)ensure that the management system of the aerodrome addresses the coordination and interface with the safety procedures of other organisations operating or providing services at the aerodrome; and#

2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(b)ensure that such organisations have safety procedures in place to comply with the applicable requirements of Regulation (EC) No 216/2008 and its Implementing Rules and the requirements laid down in the aerodrome manual.#

2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#The aerodrome operator shall:#
2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(a)establish
column3: lead and implement programmes to promote safety and the exchange of safety-relevant information; and#

column2: 2014#Regulation (EU) No 139/2014#(b)encourage organisations operating or providing services at the aerodrome to be involved in such programmes.#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#AMC1 ADR.OR.D.027 Safety programmes#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#SAFETY PROGRAMMES — AERODROME SAFETY COMMITTEES#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(a)The aerodrome operator should:#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)organise
column3: coordinate and implement programmes to promote safety at the aerodrome. Such programmes should include
column4: but are not limited to:#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(i)runway safety
column3: including runway incursion and excursion prevention;#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(ii)apron safety; and#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(iii)FOD prevention;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)coordinate and promote the exchange of information
column3: and the joint investigation of occurrences
column4: serious incidents

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(b)The aerodrome operator should establish
column3: coordinate
column4: and lead local aerodrome safety committees
column5: and a Local Runway Safety Team
column7: apron safety
column8: and the safety of the operations at the aerodrome in general. All relevant organisations operating or providing services at the aerodrome should participate to such aerodrome safety committees and the Local Runway Safety Team.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#The local aerodrome safety committees and the Local Runway Safety Team should convene regularly
column3: identify and review local safety issues
column4: and examine possible solutions
column5: and need for action. Minutes of such meetings should be kept. Procedures relevant to the functioning of local aerodrome safety committees and the Local Runway Safety Team should be included in the aerodrome manual.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#GM1 ADR.OR.D.027 Safety programmes#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#AERODROME SAFETY COMMITTEES#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(a)Manoeuvring area/Apron Safety Committee#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)The aerodrome operator should establish (a) Manoeuvring area/Apron Safety Committee(s);#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)The Manoeuvring area/Apron Safety Committee(s) should have an advisory role to the aerodrome operator;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(b)Management of Manoeuvring area /Apron Safety Committee(s)#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)The Manoeuvring area /Apron Safety Committee(s) should be chaired by an aerodrome operator’s official
column3: responsible for aerodrome operations; and#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)The aerodrome operator’s safety manager should act as the secretary of the Committee(s).#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(c)Composition of Manoeuvring area /Apron Safety Committee(s)#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#Participation should include
column3: but not limited to representatives of:#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(1)aerodrome users active in flight operations;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(2)aircraft ground handling services providers;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(3)aerodrome rescue and firefighting services;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(4)aerodrome operations;#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(5)aerodrome wildlife management;#

2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#ED Decision 2014/012/R#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#AERODROME MANUAL#
2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(a)Form of the aerodrome manual#

2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#The aerodrome manual is a key document both for the aerodrome operator and the Competent Authority. The manual is the source document describing how the aerodrome infrastructure
column3: facilities
column4: and operational procedures will operate safely.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#As well as the operational procedures
column3: the Competent Authority will expect the aerodrome manual to be an accurate reflection of the day-to-day functioning of the aerodrome's safety management system
column4: and its safety culture. It will need to show how the aerodrome intends to measure its performance against safety targets and objectives. The reader of an aerodrome manual should be given a clear statement of how safety is developed
column6: and maintained on the aerodrome. All safety policies
column7: operational procedures and instructions should be contained in detail when relevant or cross-referenced to other controlled
column9: publications.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#At larger aerodromes
column3: the size and complexity of operations
column4: and related procedures may dictate that these procedures could not easily be included in a single document. In such circumstances
column5: it is acceptable to identify and reference within the aerodrome manual the procedures which are not included within it. If this system is to be successful
column6: it is essential that any referenced information
column7: documentation
column8: and procedures are made available as necessary to all operational staff in a similar way as the aerodrome manual itself. For that purpose
column9: a computerised database containing the referenced procedures and information could be suitable. For many small aerodromes
column10: the aerodrome manual can be both simple and brief as long as it covers procedures essential for satisfactory day-to-day operations. Nevertheless
column11: it is possible to adopt a common format embracing the essential elements that define a safety management system.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#(b)Purpose of the aerodrome manual#

2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#An efficient management structure and a systematic approach to aerodrome operation is essential. The aerodrome manual should contain all the relevant information to describe this structure satisfactorily. It is one of the means by which all relevant operating staff can be informed as to their duties and responsibilities with regard to safety. It should describe the aerodrome infrastructure
column3: services and facilities
column4: all operating procedures
column5: and any restrictions on aerodrome availability.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#Accountability for safety must start at the very top of any organisation. One of the key elements in establishing safe working practices is the ‘top down’ approach where all staff should understand the safety aims of the organisation
column3: the chain of command
column4: and their own responsibilities and accountabilities. As safety management principles are applied
column5: the aerodrome manual should be expanded to describe clearly how the safety of operations is to be managed. To a reader or user of the aerodrome manual
column6: there should never be any doubt in terms of ‘safety accountability’ for each domain or activity described. Each section should define who is accountable
column7: who is responsible
column9: who has the expertise
column10: and who actually carries out the tasks described in any section.#

column2: 2014#ED Decision 2014/012/R#The principle objective of an aerodrome manual should be to show how management will accomplish its safety responsibilities. The aerodrome manual will set out the policy and expected standards of performance
column3: and the procedures by which they will be achieved.#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#GM1 ADR.OR.F.025(d) Changes#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#ASSESSMENT OF CHANGES#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(a)Safety (risk) assessment of a change#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#A safety (risk) assessment of a change includes:#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(1)the identification of the scope of the change;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(2)the identification of hazards;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(3)the determination of the safety criteria applicable to the change;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(4)the risk assessment in relation to the harmful effects or improvements in safety related to the change and
column3: if required
column4: risk mitigation for the change to meet the applicable safety criteria;#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(5)the verification that the change conforms to the scope that was subject to the safety assessment
column3: and that it meets the safety criteria
column4: before the change is put into operation; and#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(6)the specification of the monitoring requirements necessary to ensure that the aerodrome and its operation will continue to meet the safety criteria after the change has been put into operation.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(b)Scope of the safety assessment#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#The scope of the safety assessment includes the following elements and their interaction:#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(1)the aerodrome
column3: its operation
column4: management

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(2)the interfaces and interactions between the elements being changed and the rest of the system;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(3)the interfaces and interactions between the elements being changed and the environment in which they are intended to operate; and#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(4)the full lifecycle of the change from conception to operations.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(c)Safety criteria#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#The safety criteria used for the safety assessment of a change:#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(1)are compatible or the same with the safety criteria of the aerodrome operator and the air traffic services (ATS) provider;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(2)are defined in accordance with the procedures for the management of the change contained in the management system manual; and#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(3)depending on the availability of data
column3: are specified with reference to explicit quantitative acceptable safety risk levels
column4: recognised standards and/or codes of practice
column5: the safety performance of the existing or a similar system.#

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#ADR.OR.F.045 Management system#
2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(a)The organisation responsible for the provision of AMS
column3: the aerodrome operator or the air traffic service provider
column4: when the latter is partially or exclusively providing apron management services
column5: shall implement and maintain a management system that integrates a safety management system that also covers those activities.#

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(b)The management system shall include:#
2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(1)clearly defined lines of responsibility and accountability throughout the organisation
column3: including a direct accountability for safety on the part of the senior management;#

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(2)a description of the overall philosophies and principles of the organisation responsible for the provision of AMS with regard to safety
column3: referred to as the safety policy
column4: signed by the accountable manager;#

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(3)a formal process that ensures that hazards in operations are identified;#
2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(4)a formal process that ensures analysis
column3: assessment and mitigation of the safety risks in the provision of apron management service;#

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(5)the means to verify the safety performance of the organisation responsible for the provision of AMS in reference to the safety performance indicators and safety performance targets of the safety management system
column3: and to validate the effectiveness of safety risk controls;#

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(6)a formal process to:#
2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(i)identify changes within the organisation
column3: its management system
column4: or the provision of apron management service which may affect established processes

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(ii)describe the arrangements to ensure safety performance before implementing changes;#
2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(iii)eliminate or modify safety risk controls that are no longer needed or effective due to changes in the operational environment;#

column3: identify the cause(s) of substandard performance of the safety management system

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(8)a safety training programme that ensures that personnel involved in the provision of apron management service are trained and competent to perform the safety management duties;#
2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(9)formal means for safety communication that ensures that personnel are fully aware of the safety management system
column3: conveys safety-critical information
column4: and explains why particular safety actions are taken and why safety procedures are introduced or changed;#

column2: 2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(10)a formal process to monitor the compliance of the organisation with the relevant requirements.#
2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(c)The organisation responsible for the provision of AMS shall document all management system key processes in a manual.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#AMC1 ADR.OR.F.045(b)(1) Management system#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#SAFETY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM#

2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#The management system of the organisation responsible for the provision of AMS should encompass safety by establishing an organisational structure for the management of safety which is proportionate and appropriate to the size of the organisation and the complexity and type of its operations.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#Depending on the size of the organisation and the type and complexity of its operations
column3: the safety management system should include the establishment of internal safety committees.#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#GM1 ADR.OR.F.045(b)(1) Management system#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#INTERNAL SAFETY COMMITTEES#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#Organisations responsible for the provision of AMS may find it beneficial to establish a Safety Review Board and Safety Action Groups
column3: and depending on their organisational complexity and structure
column4: Safety Services Office to support Safety Manager in the execution of the assigned tasks
column5: especially in cases where the organisation provides AMS in multiple aerodromes.#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#GM2 ADR.OR.F.045(b)(1) Management system#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#SAFETY SERVICES OFFICE#

2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(a)The Safety Services Office is managed by the Safety Manager and is independent and neutral in terms of the processes and decisions made regarding the provision of services by the operational unit(s).#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(b)The functions of the Safety Services Office normally support the Safety Manager in the following:#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(1)the management and oversight of the hazard identification system;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(2)the monitoring of the safety performance of the operational unit(s) that is (are) directly involved in the provision of apron management services (AMS);#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(3)the provision of advice to the senior management on safety management matters; and#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(4)the provision of assistance to line managers on safety management matters.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#GM3 ADR.OR.F.045(b)(1) Management system#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#SAFETY REVIEW BOARD AND SAFETY ACTION GROUP#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(a)The Safety Review Board:#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(1)is a high-level safety committee that considers matters of strategic safety in support of the accountable manager’s safety accountability; and#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(2)is chaired by the accountable manager and composed of heads of functional areas.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(b)The Safety Review Board monitors:#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(1)the organisation’s safety performance against the safety policy and safety objectives;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(2)that any safety action is taken in a timely manner; and#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(3)the effectiveness of the organisation’s safety management processes.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(c)The Safety Review Board ensures that appropriate resources are allocated for the organisation to achieve the safety objectives.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(d)The safety manager or any other relevant person
column3: as appropriate
column4: may attend the Safety Review Board meetings. They may communicate to the accountable manager all the relevant information
column6: to allow decision-making based on safety data.#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(e)Depending on the size of the organisation and the type and complexity of its operations
column3: the responsibilities of the Safety Review Board may be transferred in other high-level committees of the organisation.#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(f)The Safety Action Group#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(1)The Safety Action Group may be established as a standing group
column3: or as an ad hoc group
column4: to assist or act on behalf of the Safety Review Board;#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(2)More than one Safety Action Group may be established
column3: depending on the scope of the task and the specific expertise required;#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(3)The Safety Action Group reports to
column3: and takes strategic direction from
column4: the Safety Review Board
column7: and personnel from operational areas.#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(4)The Safety Action Group:#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(i)monitors operational safety;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(ii)resolves identified risks;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(iii)assesses the impact of operational services on safety;#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(iv)ensures that safety actions are implemented within the agreed timescales.#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(5)The Safety Action Group reviews the effectiveness of previous safety recommendations and safety promotion activities.#

column3: the following tools and techniques may be used:#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(ii)the hazard and operability (HAZOP) study
column3: which is a systematic and structured approach using parameter and deviation guidewords. This technique relies on a very detailed system description being available for study
column4: and usually involves breaking down the system into well-defined subsystems and functional or process flows between subsystems. Each element of the system is then subject to discussion within a multidisciplinary group of experts against the various combinations of the guidewords and deviations;#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(iii)checklists
column3: which are lists of known hazards or hazard causes that have been derived from past experience. Past experience could be previous risk assessments
column4: or similar systems
column6: or from actual incidents that have occurred in the past. This technique involves the systematic use of an appropriate checklist
column7: and the consideration of each item on the checklist for possible applicability to a particular system. Checklists are always validated for applicability prior to use;#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(iv)the failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA)
column3: which is a ‘bottom-up’ technique used to consider ways in which the basic components of a system can fail to perform their design intent. This technique relies on a detailed system description
column4: and considers the ways in which each subcomponent of the system could fail to meet its design intent
column5: and what the consequences could be for the overall system. For each subcomponent of a system

2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#GM1 ADR.OR.F.095 Management system manual#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#MANAGEMENT SYSTEM MANUAL#
2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(a)Form of the manual#

2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#The management system manual is a key document both for the organisation responsible for the provision of AMS and the Competent Authority. The manual should reflect accurately the day-to-day functioning of the organisation’s safety management system and safety culture. It will need to show how the organisation intends to measure its performance against the safety targets and objectives. The reader or the user of the manual should be given a clear statement of how safety is developed
column3: managed and maintained in the organisation. All safety policies and instructions should be presented in detail and
column4: when relevant

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#(b)Purpose of the manual#

2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#The manual contains all the relevant information to describe the management system structure satisfactorily. It is one of the means by which all relevant operational staff can be informed about their duties and responsibilities with regard to safety.#

2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#Accountability for safety must start at the very top of the organisation. One of the key elements in establishing safe working practices is the ‘top-down’ approach where all staff understand the safety objectives of the organisation
column3: the chain of command and their own responsibilities and accountabilities within the organisation. As safety management principles are applied
column4: the manual is expanded to describe clearly how the safety of operations is to be managed. The user of the manual should never be in any doubt in terms of ‘safety accountability’ for each domain or activity described. Each section should define who is accountable
column7: who has the expertise
column8: and who actually carries out the tasks described in any of the sections.#

column2: 2020#ED Decision 2020/021/R#The principal objective of the manual should be to show how management will fulfil its safety responsibilities. The manual will set out the policy and the expected standards of performance
column3: and the procedures by which they will be achieved.#

2020#Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/1234#(c)disseminate operational instructions and other information without delay.#
